text:
This study examined the historical developments, impacts and challenges theological training has encountered in The Apostolic Church Nigeria (TACN) from 1931 to the present. It examined the developments in the schools of the church and assessed how the training given to members and ministers affected the doctrine of the church and the ministry. It also looked at the challenges that impeded the growth and development of the schools. Using qualitative, historical research methodology, the study analysed the materials provided by the school management, and also gathered more information from selected instructors, graduates and students. These were to give a complete understanding of the historical development of theological training in TACN.

The study finds that institutions such as theological colleges in Amumara and Jos have been instrumental in formalizing pastoral education, enhancing the intellectual and spiritual competence of religious leaders and also leading to the growth of churches. Nevertheless, the study highlights several challenges that include economic hardship, cultural reluctance to embrace formal education as well as the ongoing struggle between maintaining a spiritually vibrant minister and at the same time becoming an academically efficient one. Therefore, the study recommends that infrastructure development, faculty capacity building, the use of information and communication technology and curriculum review in order to cope with emerging trends, amongst others, are urgently required for the sustenance of the present educational structure at TACN and consequently the Pentecostal Churches in Nigeria. In all, the study strongly advocates for an integrated educational structure that blends both academic pursuits and spiritual development. This study can serve as a reference point for other Pentecostal denominations that are also expanding their institutions of learning within their folds.
